text:
Cataracts

A cataract is a clouding of the eye's natural lens, which sits behind the iris and pupil. The lens focuses light onto the retina to produce clear images. When the lens becomes cloudy, light cannot pass through as easily, and vision becomes blurred or hazy. Cataracts are the leading cause of blindness worldwide and account for a significant proportion of visual impairment in Africa, wher

de and account for a significant proportion of visual impairment in Africa, where access to surgical treatment remains limited in many areas.

Cataracts develop gradually and are most commonly associated with aging. The proteins in the lens can clump together over time, forming cloudy areas that interfere with clear vision. Risk factors include advancing age, prolonged exposure to ultraviolet (UV)

sion. Risk factors include advancing age, prolonged exposure to ultraviolet (UV) sunlight, smoking, excessive alcohol consumption, diabetes, long-term use of corticosteroid medications, and previous eye injury or surgery. Cataracts can also be present at birth (congenital cataracts) or develop in childhood due to infection, injury, or metabolic conditions.

Common symptoms of cataracts include blu

tion, injury, or metabolic conditions.

Common symptoms of cataracts include blurred or cloudy vision, fading or yellowing of colours, difficulty with night vision, increased sensitivity to glare from lights, and seeing halos around lights. Double vision in one eye and frequent changes to eyeglass or contact lens prescriptions may also occur. In the early stages, stronger lighting and updated glas

riptions may also occur. In the early stages, stronger lighting and updated glasses may help manage symptoms. However, as the cataract progresses, these measures become less effective.

The only effective treatment for cataracts is surgery, which involves removing the cloudy lens and replacing it with a clear artificial lens. Cataract surgery is one of the most commonly performed and successful su

l lens. Cataract surgery is one of the most commonly performed and successful surgical procedures globally. It is typically an outpatient procedure and, where available, has a high rate of restoring useful vision. In many lower-income settings, community eye health programmes work to increase access to cataract surgery as a public health intervention.

Protecting the eyes from UV radiation by wear

as a public health intervention.

Protecting the eyes from UV radiation by wearing sunglasses and broad-brimmed hats, avoiding smoking, and managing conditions such as diabetes can help reduce the risk of developing cataracts earlier in life.

Conjunctivitis (Pink Eye)

Conjunctivitis, commonly called pink eye, is an inflammation or infection of the conjunctiva — the thin, transparent layer of tissue that lines the inner surface of the eyelid and covers the white part of the eye. It is one of the most common eye conditions worldwide and can affect people of all ages.

There are three main types of conjunctivitis. Viral conjunctivitis is

all ages.

There are three main types of conjunctivitis. Viral conjunctivitis is the most common form and is often associated with the common cold or upper respiratory infections. It typically causes a watery or mucous discharge, redness, and a gritty feeling in one or both eyes. Bacterial conjunctivitis produces a thicker, yellowish-green discharge that may cause the eyelids to stick together, es

cker, yellowish-green discharge that may cause the eyelids to stick together, especially after sleep. Allergic conjunctivitis is triggered by allergens such as pollen, dust mites, or animal dander, and usually affects both eyes simultaneously, causing intense itching, tearing, and swelling.

Common symptoms of conjunctivitis include redness in the white of the eye, increased tearing, a discharge t

ivitis include redness in the white of the eye, increased tearing, a discharge that may form a crust during sleep, itching or burning sensation, and a feeling that something is in the eye. In most cases, conjunctivitis does not affect vision. However, light sensitivity may occur with some forms of the condition.

Conjunctivitis caused by viruses or bacteria can be highly contagious and spreads thr

nctivitis caused by viruses or bacteria can be highly contagious and spreads through direct or indirect contact with the secretions of an infected person. Good hygiene practices, such as frequent handwashing, avoiding touching the eyes, and not sharing towels or pillowcases, are important in preventing the spread of infectious conjunctivitis. Allergic conjunctivitis is not contagious.

Most cases

fectious conjunctivitis. Allergic conjunctivitis is not contagious.

Most cases of conjunctivitis resolve on their own within one to two weeks without specific treatment. Applying a clean, warm or cool compress to closed eyes may help relieve discomfort. Anyone experiencing significant pain, sensitivity to light, blurred vision, or symptoms that worsen or do not improve should seek assessment from
ed vision, or symptoms that worsen or do not improve should seek assessment from a healthcare professional.

Diabetic Retinopathy

Diabetic retinopathy is a diabetes-related eye complication that affects the blood vessels of the retina — the light-sensitive tissue at the back of the eye. It is a leading cause of vision loss and blindness among adults of working age globally. The condition develops when prolonged high blood sugar levels damage the small blood vessels that supply the retina, causing them t

gar levels damage the small blood vessels that supply the retina, causing them to leak, swell, or grow abnormally.

Diabetic retinopathy progresses through two main stages. In the earlier stage, known as non-proliferative diabetic retinopathy (NPDR), the walls of the retinal blood vessels weaken and may leak fluid or blood into the retina. This can cause swelling in the central part of the retina

lood into the retina. This can cause swelling in the central part of the retina (the macula), a condition called diabetic macular oedema (DMO), which affects central and detailed vision. In the more advanced stage, proliferative diabetic retinopathy (PDR), new and fragile blood vessels grow abnormally on the surface of the retina. These vessels bleed easily and can lead to scar tissue formation, w

the retina. These vessels bleed easily and can lead to scar tissue formation, which may pull on the retina and cause retinal detachment.

In the early stages, diabetic retinopathy often produces no symptoms or only mild changes in vision that can be easily overlooked. Symptoms that may appear as the condition advances include blurred or fluctuating vision, dark spots or floaters in the field of v

include blurred or fluctuating vision, dark spots or floaters in the field of vision, difficulty seeing colours, and sudden vision loss. Because early-stage disease is often asymptomatic, people with diabetes are recommended to have regular dilated eye examinations — at least once a year — regardless of whether they notice any vision changes.

Good management of diabetes is the most important fac

otice any vision changes.

Good management of diabetes is the most important factor in preventing or slowing the progression of diabetic retinopathy. Maintaining blood sugar, blood pressure, and blood cholesterol within recommended ranges significantly reduces the risk. Avoiding smoking also lowers risk. When detected early, treatment options including laser therapy and injections into the eye can

early, treatment options including laser therapy and injections into the eye can help prevent severe vision loss, though they do not cure the underlying condition.

People with diabetes who notice any sudden change in their vision should seek prompt assessment from an eye care professional. Regular screening is essential, as early intervention is far more effective than treating advanced disease.
al, as early intervention is far more effective than treating advanced disease.

General Eye Health and Hygiene

The eyes are complex and delicate organs. Protecting them through everyday habits and regular professional care is one of the most effective ways to maintain good vision throughout life. Many of the leading causes of visual impairment globally are preventable or treatable when identified early through routine eye examinations.

Adults are generally recommended to ha

early through routine eye examinations.

Adults are generally recommended to have a comprehensive eye examination at least every two years, or more frequently if they have risk factors such as diabetes, a family history of eye disease, or existing eye conditions. Children should have their vision assessed as part of routine health checks, since conditions such as amblyopia (lazy eye), strabismus

outine health checks, since conditions such as amblyopia (lazy eye), strabismus (squint), and refractive errors are more effectively treated the earlier they are identified. In many African health systems, eye screening is integrated into child health services and school programmes.

Protective habits that support eye health include wearing sunglasses that block ultraviolet A and B (UVA and UVB) r

health include wearing sunglasses that block ultraviolet A and B (UVA and UVB) radiation, especially in bright outdoor conditions. UV exposure over time is associated with cataract development and damage to the retina. Eye protection should also be worn during activities involving dust, chemicals, tools, or sport where there is a risk of eye injury. At work or school, following occupational health

there is a risk of eye injury. At work or school, following occupational health guidelines for screen use — including taking regular short breaks and ensuring adequate lighting — can reduce eye strain.

Nutrition contributes to eye health. Diets rich in leafy green vegetables, colourful fruits, and foods containing omega-3 fatty acids (such as fish) provide nutrients including lutein, zeaxanthin,

ega-3 fatty acids (such as fish) provide nutrients including lutein, zeaxanthin, and vitamins C and E that are associated with a lower risk of age-related eye conditions. Adequate hydration also supports the tear film that keeps the eye surface comfortable.

Contact lens users should follow hygiene instructions carefully: washing hands before handling lenses, using the correct lens solutions, not

ly: washing hands before handling lenses, using the correct lens solutions, not wearing lenses longer than recommended, and avoiding sleeping in lenses unless specifically prescribed for overnight wear. Improper contact lens use is a common cause of preventable eye infections.

Smoking is a significant risk factor for several serious eye conditions, including age-related macular degeneration, cata

several serious eye conditions, including age-related macular degeneration, cataracts, and diabetic retinopathy. Stopping smoking at any age reduces the risk of these conditions developing or worsening.

Glaucoma

Glaucoma is a group of eye conditions that damage the optic nerve, which carries visual information from the eye to the brain. It is a leading cause of irreversible blindness globally and disproportionately affects people of African descent, who have a higher lifetime risk and tend to develop the condition at a younger age. Most forms of glaucoma are associated with elevated intraocular

a younger age. Most forms of glaucoma are associated with elevated intraocular pressure (IOP), though the condition can occur at normal pressure levels as well.

The most common form is primary open-angle glaucoma, which develops slowly and without pain. In its early stages, it typically causes no noticeable symptoms because peripheral (side) vision is affected first, and the brain compensates fo

because peripheral (side) vision is affected first, and the brain compensates for the loss. By the time central vision is affected, significant and irreversible damage may already have occurred. This is why glaucoma is often called the "silent thief of sight." Angle-closure glaucoma, a less common but more acute form, can cause sudden eye pain, headache, nausea, blurred vision, and halos around li

can cause sudden eye pain, headache, nausea, blurred vision, and halos around lights and requires urgent medical attention.

Risk factors for glaucoma include increasing age, elevated intraocular pressure, a family history of glaucoma, African or Hispanic ethnicity, previous eye injury, prolonged use of corticosteroid medications, and certain medical conditions such as diabetes and hypertension. R

medications, and certain medical conditions such as diabetes and hypertension. Regular eye examinations that include measurement of intraocular pressure and assessment of the optic nerve are the primary way to detect glaucoma before significant vision loss occurs.

Although glaucoma cannot be cured and any vision already lost cannot be restored, progression can be slowed or halted with appropriate

dy lost cannot be restored, progression can be slowed or halted with appropriate management. Treatment options include prescription eye drops to reduce intraocular pressure, laser procedures, and surgery. Adherence to prescribed treatment is essential, as glaucoma is a lifelong condition requiring ongoing monitoring.

Routine eye examinations are especially important for individuals with risk fact

Routine eye examinations are especially important for individuals with risk factors. People with a family history of glaucoma, those over 40, and individuals with known elevated eye pressure should discuss the frequency of screening with an eye care professional.

Retinoblastoma

Retinoblastoma is a rare type of eye cancer that develops in the retina, the light-sensitive tissue at the back of the eye. It is the most common eye cancer in children and almost always occurs in children under five years of age. It can affect one eye (unilateral) or both eyes (bilateral). Bilateral cases are more likely to be hereditary.

The most recognisable early sign of retin

ses are more likely to be hereditary.

The most recognisable early sign of retinoblastoma is leukocoria, often described as a white or cat's eye reflex visible in the pupil — particularly noticeable in photographs taken with a flash, where one eye reflects red and the affected eye appears white. Other signs may include a noticeable outward or inward turning of one eye (strabismus), redness or swel

a noticeable outward or inward turning of one eye (strabismus), redness or swelling of the eye, and reduced or changed vision. Because young children cannot communicate vision changes, parents and caregivers play an important role in noticing these signs.

Retinoblastoma originates from a mutation in the RB1 gene, which normally helps regulate cell growth. In hereditary retinoblastoma, this mutat

ch normally helps regulate cell growth. In hereditary retinoblastoma, this mutation is present in all cells of the body and can be passed from parent to child. Children from families with a known history of retinoblastoma may be recommended for regular screening examinations even before birth. In non-hereditary cases, the mutation occurs only in the retinal cells.

Outcomes for children with retin

the mutation occurs only in the retinal cells.

Outcomes for children with retinoblastoma are generally good when the condition is detected and treated early. Treatment options depend on the size, location, and stage of the tumour, and may include laser therapy, cryotherapy, chemotherapy, radiation, or in some cases removal of the eye (enucleation) to prevent the spread of cancer. Advances in trea

moval of the eye (enucleation) to prevent the spread of cancer. Advances in treatment have significantly improved survival rates, particularly in higher-income settings.

Early detection is critical. Parents who notice a white pupil reflex, unusual eye alignment, or any other eye change in their child should seek urgent assessment from a healthcare professional. In many African countries, awarenes
t assessment from a healthcare professional. In many African countries, awareness campaigns are actively working to improve early presentation and treatment access.

Trachoma

Trachoma is a bacterial eye infection caused by Chlamydia trachomatis. It is the leading infectious cause of blindness worldwide and a significant public health concern, particularly in communities with limited access to clean water, sanitation, and healthcare. The World Health Organization (WHO) classifies trachoma as a neglected tropical disease.

The infection is spread through direct

rachoma as a neglected tropical disease.

The infection is spread through direct contact with eye or nose discharge from an infected person, contact with contaminated objects such as towels and clothing, and through certain flies that are attracted to eye and nose secretions. Children are most susceptible to infection, and repeated episodes throughout childhood can lead to progressive damage to th

and repeated episodes throughout childhood can lead to progressive damage to the eyelid and eye surface. Poverty, overcrowded living conditions, and inadequate face hygiene are major risk factors.

Trachoma progresses through several stages. In the early stages, the inner surface of the upper eyelid becomes inflamed and develops small follicles. With repeated infections, the inside of the eyelid

nd develops small follicles. With repeated infections, the inside of the eyelid scars and may eventually turn inward — a condition called trichiasis — causing the eyelashes to rub against the cornea. Over time, this repeated abrasion can scar the cornea, leading to visual impairment and, if untreated, irreversible blindness.

The WHO promotes a SAFE strategy for trachoma control: Surgery to correc

dness.

The WHO promotes a SAFE strategy for trachoma control: Surgery to correct advanced trichiasis, Antibiotics to treat active infection (azithromycin is commonly used in community-level programs), Facial cleanliness to reduce transmission, and Environmental improvement including access to water and sanitation. Regular face washing, particularly in children, is one of the most effective indivi

ular face washing, particularly in children, is one of the most effective individual-level prevention measures.

Trachoma is preventable and treatable, especially when identified early. Communities in endemic regions benefit from public health programs targeting hygiene education and mass antibiotic distribution. Anyone in an affected region who notices persistent eye irritation, discharge, or eye
e in an affected region who notices persistent eye irritation, discharge, or eyelid changes should seek assessment from a healthcare provider.

When to Seek Urgent Eye Care

Many eye conditions can be managed with routine appointments, but some symptoms require prompt or emergency assessment. Delays in seeking care for certain eye conditions can result in permanent, irreversible vision loss. Knowing which symptoms warrant urgent attention is an important part of protecting eye health.

Symptoms that require same-day or emergency assessmen

of protecting eye health.

Symptoms that require same-day or emergency assessment include sudden loss of vision in one or both eyes, sudden appearance of a large number of new floaters (dark spots, threads, or cobweb-like shapes in the field of vision), flashes of light particularly in the periphery of vision, a shadow or curtain moving across the visual field, sudden eye pain especially when acco

or curtain moving across the visual field, sudden eye pain especially when accompanied by nausea or vomiting, chemical or physical injury to the eye, and a white or cloudy appearance in the pupil of a child's eye. These symptoms may indicate serious conditions such as retinal detachment, acute angle-closure glaucoma, or retinoblastoma in children, all of which require rapid treatment to preserve

or retinoblastoma in children, all of which require rapid treatment to preserve vision.

Red eye with significant pain, sensitivity to light, and decreased vision — rather than the mild irritation typical of common conjunctivitis — also requires urgent professional assessment. Similarly, an eye that has been struck, cut, or had a foreign body enter it should be assessed promptly. The eye should no

t, or had a foreign body enter it should be assessed promptly. The eye should not be rubbed or pressed if penetrating injury is suspected.

For chemical injuries, immediate first aid is to irrigate the eye with large amounts of clean water for at least 15 to 20 minutes before seeking emergency care. Acid and alkali burns can cause severe and permanent damage, and rapid irrigation is one of the mos

ns can cause severe and permanent damage, and rapid irrigation is one of the most important steps in limiting harm.

Routine but non-urgent symptoms — such as gradual blurring of vision, mild redness without pain, difficulty reading, or increased glare sensitivity — should still be assessed by an eye care professional within days to weeks rather than being ignored. Many common eye conditions respo

within days to weeks rather than being ignored. Many common eye conditions respond well to treatment when identified early, but may cause lasting harm if left unaddressed. Anyone who is unsure whether their symptoms require urgent care should contact a healthcare professional for guidance.
